_comment: Newest update FIRST. The first item becomes 'This Week'. To post a new week, copy one block to the top and edit it. 'image' is optional (a filename in images/ or a full URL); leave it as an empty string to use the gradient placeholder.

updates.title: Design begins on the Susie Stephens connection
updates.summary: With easements donated and $2M of the $3.5M secured, the Town is poised to begin design on the 950-foot segment from the Susie Stephens Trail to Confluence Park.
updates.body:
This is the stretch that does the most for everyday safety. Segment 2 runs 950 feet from the Susie Stephens Trail to Confluence Park — the piece that finally lets people walk from the trail system into downtown without stepping onto the Highway 20 fog line.

Two things made this week possible. Landowners along the route have generously donated easements, so the path can cross private property and stay close to the water. And $2 million of the segment's $3.5 million cost is already in hand through the Recreation and Conservation Office's WWRP Trails program.

Next up: the Town begins design work, locating the six-foot path and its micro-pile supports along the bank.

updates.title: A record number of neighbors weigh in
updates.summary: 521 people answered the Town's parks survey — the most ever — and ranked the Riverwalk the #1 park project, regardless of where they live.
updates.body:
The 2024 parks survey drew 521 responses, by far the most in the history of doing these surveys (there were 152 back in 2017). When asked to rank six possible park projects, respondents put the Riverwalk first — Winthrop residents, the wider Methow Valley, and visitors from outside the valley all agreed.

It's a clear mandate. People want safe, walkable connections through town, and they want this trail. The Riverwalk also ranks #2 on Okanogan County's countywide infrastructure priority list.

updates.title: Why 560 feet matters
updates.summary: Today, pedestrians are forced onto the Highway 20 fog line beside motorcycles, cars, and logging trucks. The first stretch of Riverwalk removes that danger.
updates.body:
Stand at the gap between the downtown boardwalks and the Susie Stephens Trail and you'll see the problem. For about 560 feet, anyone on foot — families, elders, kids on bikes — shares the shoulder of Highway 20 with traffic that ranges from passenger cars to semis and logging trucks.

The Riverwalk closes that gap. Instead of the fog line, walkers get a safe, dedicated path along the river. That single connection opens up pedestrian access to stores, health care, the bike and ski trails, Mack Lloyd Park, the Winthrop Rink, the new library, and Little Star Montessori School.

updates.title: The Chewuch Bridge underpass is funded for 2026
updates.summary: Segment 1 — an underpass of the Chewuch River Bridge — is funded and planned for construction this year.
updates.body:
Good news on the first segment. The underpass of the Chewuch River Bridge is funded and slated for construction in 2026, carrying the route safely beneath a busy crossing.

It's a modest length of trail with an outsized role: it's the first physical piece of Riverwalk to go in the ground, and it sets the pattern for the segments that follow — a six-foot, hard-surfaced path built on micro piles so it can navigate uneven terrain and changes in elevation while protecting the native vegetation and habitat along the bank.

updates.title: Shoring up the bank at the confluence
updates.summary: The project will replace failing bank stabilization where the Chewuch meets the Methow, paired with riparian habitat restoration.
updates.body:
The Riverwalk isn't only about people — it's about the river, too. Where the Chewuch and Methow rivers come together, existing bank stabilization is failing. The project will replace it, and add riparian habitat restoration to support native species both within and adjacent to the river channel.

Doing the trail and the restoration together means the path strengthens the shoreline instead of stressing it — a healthier riverbank that will hold for the long run.

updates.title: Easements make the route possible
updates.summary: The Riverwalk crosses private land — and it's the generosity of landowners donating easements that lets the trail hug the shoreline.
updates.body:
A trail along the downtown riverfront has to cross private property. The reason it can is simple and generous: the owners of the land are donating easements so the public can walk the shoreline.

Those gifts of land are as valuable as any grant. They keep the Riverwalk close to the water — where the views of the North Cascades open up and the river runs wild, right in the heart of town — and they shave time and cost off the path to construction.

updates.title: A 30-year vision takes its first steps
updates.summary: The Town of Winthrop and the Friends of the Riverwalk are partnering to build a continuous, free path along the Chewuch and Methow rivers.
updates.body:
For decades, local business owners and community leaders have imagined a public pathway opening up the Chewuch River shoreline in downtown Winthrop. Now that 30-year vision is unfolding: the Town of Winthrop and the Friends of the Riverwalk are partnering to build it.

The Riverwalk will enhance safety and mobility, support local businesses, and let residents and visitors in on one of Winthrop's best-kept secrets — a stretch of wild river with magnificent views, in the middle of town. We'll post here every week so you can follow every step. Welcome to the walk.
